decision: Leonardo suppressed his underwater breathing apparatus from publication despite its technical brilliance, releasing only the safe variant.
context: While developing diving technology, Leonardo invented both a surface breathing tube and a fully submerged air-supply system. He was in chronic need of patronage and recognition, and publishing novel inventions was one of the few ways an unliterary polymath could build reputation. Venice was at war with the Ottoman Turks, creating immediate military demand for underwater capabilities. The conventional alternative was to publish everything and let patrons decide on deployment.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Leonardo attended to the second-order weaponization potential of the device, not just its engineering elegance. Where a conventional military engineer would have seen a deliverable that pleased a patron, Leonardo perceived the full chain of misuse: assassination by underwater approach, the sinking of ships with crews aboard. He noticed that the danger was not in the device's primary function but in its accessibility once published, attending to the information-theoretic problem (once disclosed, it cannot be un-disclosed) rather than the immediate tactical one.
cdm_probes.active_goals: On the surface, Leonardo wanted to advance knowledge and serve patrons. But his operative goal was to maintain a personal ethical boundary around lethal innovation, specifically distinguishing between tools that extend human capability and tools that enable covert killing. He was also protecting his own moral position: he wanted to be an inventor, not an arms dealer whose weapons killed invisibly.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: First, full publication of all diving technology: rejected because once the submerged breathing method was public, no amount of moral disclaimer could prevent its weaponization. The evaluative criterion was irreversibility of information release. Second, publishing with a warning or restricted audience: rejected because Leonardo understood that written warnings do not constrain determined bad actors, revealing his criterion that only structural suppression (not normative appeals) can prevent misuse. Third, sharing only with his patron: rejected implicitly because even a single patron could propagate the knowledge or be overthrown, exposing the secret.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: A conventional military engineer would have framed this as a deliverable: build the device, present it to the patron, collect payment. Leonardo reframed it as an ethical publishing decision with irreversible consequences. He was operating from a mental model closer to modern responsible-disclosure thinking: the question was not 'can I build this?' but 'should this information exist outside my notebooks?'
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Leonardo expected to lose the recognition and patronage that would come from a dramatic military innovation. He accepted this cost as preferable to enabling a class of invisible underwater assassinations. He was calibrated to the possibility that the invention would die with him.

counterfactual: A competent Renaissance military engineer like Francesco di Giorgio Martini would have presented the full diving system to the Venetian Senate as a weapon against the Turks, viewing it as exactly the kind of innovation that justified an engineer's salary. The question of misuse would not have registered as the inventor's problem.
divergence_explanation: Leonardo's decision diverged because he operated from a causal model that extended past the immediate patron-engineer transaction. Where a conventional engineer perceived a linear chain (invent, deliver, get paid), Leonardo perceived a branching tree of downstream consequences including misuse by unknown future actors. This reflects a cognitive pattern of extended temporal and social modeling: he evaluated inventions not by their intended use but by their worst-case deployment across all possible future holders of the knowledge.
outcome: The submerged breathing apparatus was never published. It survived only as a suppressed note in his manuscripts. The safe surface-tube variant was described and circulated.

decision: Leonardo chose to write his notebooks as disordered collections rather than delay writing to organize them properly.
context: Beginning a new notebook in Florence in 1508, Leonardo faced the accumulated mass of hundreds of loose papers containing decades of observations across dozens of fields. The conventional scholarly approach was to organize material by subject before committing it to a bound volume. Leonardo was aging, had no institutional support for archiving, and knew his memory could not retain the full corpus. The cost of cross-referencing everything before each writing session would have been enormous.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Leonardo attended to the ratio of time-spent-organizing to time-spent-recording and judged the former to be the bottleneck. He noticed that his subjects were 'so many' and his memory 'incapable of retaining them,' cues that a conventional scholar would have treated as arguments for better indexing. Instead, Leonardo treated them as arguments for abandoning the indexing requirement entirely. He also noticed the risk of repetition and pre-accepted it as a cheaper cost than lost observations.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Leonardo's operative goal was to maximize the throughput of observation-to-paper transfer. His deeper structural objective was to preserve the maximum volume of his intellectual output against the constraint of his own mortality, even at the cost of redundancy and disorder. He was optimizing for capture rate, not retrieval rate.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: First, organizing by subject before writing: rejected because the cross-referencing cost per session would have reduced his capture rate below what he considered acceptable. The criterion was throughput over tidiness. Second, hiring a scribe to organize existing papers: rejected implicitly because Leonardo's mirror-script and cross-domain content made delegation impractical. Third, accepting that some observations would be lost: this was the conventional default he explicitly refused, preferring redundancy (repeating himself) to gaps.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: A conventional scholar would have framed this as a library-science problem: how to create a well-indexed, non-redundant reference work. Leonardo reframed it as a data-preservation emergency under time pressure. His mental model was closer to a field researcher dumping observations into a log than a scholar composing a treatise.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Leonardo expected to repeat himself many times and apologized to the reader in advance. He hoped to arrange the material 'later each in its place' but doubted he would reach the end of the task. He was calibrated to the likelihood that disorder would be permanent.

counterfactual: A competent Renaissance scholar like Leon Battista Alberti would have organized observations into topical treatises before publication, accepting that some material might never make it into the final work. The disorder of Leonardo's notebooks would have been viewed as a sign of intellectual immaturity.
divergence_explanation: Leonardo's decision diverged because he valued the preservation of raw observations over the presentation of finished thought. His cognitive pattern was to treat his notebooks as an extension of working memory rather than as a publication medium. Where Alberti would have filtered for coherence, Leonardo refused to filter at all, revealing a deep belief that unprocessed observations had value in themselves and that the cost of lost data exceeded the cost of lost organization.
outcome: The notebooks survived as thousands of disordered pages mixing anatomy, hydraulics, optics, painting, and personal notes. They were never organized in his lifetime and remained largely unpublished for centuries.

decision: Leonardo marketed himself to Ludovico Sforza primarily as a military engineer, burying his painting abilities as an afterthought in paragraph ten of his application letter.
context: Stuck in Florence without Medici patronage in the early 1480s, watching rivals like Botticelli receive papal commissions, Leonardo needed a new patron. Ludovico Sforza in Milan was consolidating military power and facing threats from multiple Italian states. Leonardo's established reputation was as a painter and sculptor. The conventional approach for a Florentine artist seeking Milanese patronage was to lead with artistic credentials and seek a court-painter position. Leonardo instead drafted a ten-paragraph letter emphasizing portable bridges, siege mines, covered chariots, mortars, and naval weapons.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Leonardo attended to Ludovico's strategic situation rather than his own proven strengths. He noticed that Milan's court was on a war footing and that Ludovico's primary anxiety was military survival, not cultural prestige. Where a conventional painter would have scanned for artistic commissions, Leonardo scanned for the patron's actual threat landscape and matched his pitch to it. He also noticed that his rivals in painting were well-established, while the military engineering field in Milan had weaker competition.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Leonardo's surface goal was to secure patronage in Milan. His deeper structural objective was to gain a position that would fund his cross-domain research agenda, not just painting commissions. By entering as an engineer, he could access foundries, workshops, canal projects, and military campaigns, all of which fed his investigative interests far more than a court-painter role would have.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: First, leading with painting credentials: rejected because it would have placed him in direct competition with established Milanese painters and reduced him to a specialist role. The criterion was competitive positioning. Second, a balanced presentation of all skills equally: rejected because it would have diluted the military message and failed to address Ludovico's primary anxiety. Third, seeking a different patron with more artistic interests: rejected because Leonardo wanted access to engineering-scale projects, not just commissions.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: A conventional painter would have framed this as an art-market problem: find the patron who wants paintings. Leonardo reframed it as a strategic consulting pitch: identify the patron's most urgent need and position himself as the solution, regardless of which personal capability that required foregrounding. His mental model was closer to a modern management consultant than a Renaissance artisan.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Leonardo expected to win a position that gave him access to Milan's military infrastructure, foundries, and engineering projects while also being able to paint on the side. He was willing to risk being typecast as an engineer if it got him through the door. He offered proof-by-experiment rather than credentials.

counterfactual: A competent Florentine painter like Perugino or Ghirlandaio, seeking Milanese patronage, would have sent a portfolio of completed paintings and sought introduction through artistic channels. They would have viewed leading with military engineering claims as bizarre overreach that risked credibility.
divergence_explanation: Leonardo's decision diverged because he operated from a patron-needs-first model rather than an artist-identity-first model. His cognitive pattern was to treat his own multi-domain capabilities as a portfolio to be selectively deployed based on market conditions, not as a fixed identity to be advertised. This reflects a deeper structural habit: Leonardo optimized for access to problems, not for recognition within a discipline.
outcome: Leonardo secured a position at the Milanese court that lasted nearly two decades, gaining access to the Sforza bronze horse commission, canal engineering, military projects, theatrical productions, and the Last Supper. The pitch worked precisely because it matched the patron's needs.

decision: Leonardo abandoned a galloping-horse design for the Sforza monument and substituted a pacing horse after observing that sculpted figures in motion appear to fall forward.
context: Leonardo had been awarded the prestigious commission for an enormous bronze equestrian statue of Francesco Sforza. His initial competition design featured a dramatically galloping horse with a fallen enemy beneath, which was the more ambitious and crowd-pleasing conception. He had invested significant design time in this scheme. The conventional expectation was to proceed with the winning design. However, Leonardo's own observation of how motion translates into static sculpture revealed a fundamental visual flaw.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Leonardo noticed a physical-perceptual problem that his competitors either did not see or chose to ignore: that a figure sculpted in mid-gallop, when actually standing still in bronze, would read as falling forward rather than running. This is a cue about the gap between depicted motion and static balance that requires cross-domain attention to both physics of motion and psychology of visual perception. A conventional sculptor would have trusted the dramatic power of the galloping pose to override any awkwardness.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Leonardo's operative goal was not to deliver the most dramatic statue but the most perceptually truthful one. His deeper objective was to solve the engineering-aesthetics problem correctly rather than impressively, treating the commission as a problem in applied physics of visual perception rather than a competition to be won with bravura.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: First, proceeding with the galloping design as approved: rejected because Leonardo's own observation had revealed a flaw he could not unsee, regardless of what the commissioners expected. The criterion was perceptual truth over dramatic impact. Second, modifying the galloping pose to compensate for the falling-forward effect: likely considered but rejected because the fundamental physics of a mid-gallop pose in static material could not be resolved through tweaking. Third, accepting the visual compromise as acceptable given the monument's height and viewing distance: rejected because Leonardo refused to exploit viewer ignorance of a flaw he himself understood.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: A conventional sculptor would have framed this as a delivery problem: execute the approved design at the required scale. Leonardo reframed it as a perception-physics problem: find the pose that is both monumental and perceptually stable in static bronze. His mental model integrated mechanics, optics, and sculptural tradition into a single evaluative framework.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Leonardo expected the pacing horse to be less immediately dramatic but more durably correct as a visual experience. He accepted the cost of abandoning his invested design work and potentially disappointing commissioners who preferred the galloping version.

counterfactual: Andrea del Verrocchio (Leonardo's own master) or another competent Renaissance sculptor would have proceeded with the galloping design, trusting the dramatic convention and the monument's elevated position to mask any perceptual awkwardness. Reversing course after winning a competition design would have seemed indecisive.
divergence_explanation: Leonardo's decision diverged because he applied direct observational testing to a design that conventional practice treated as settled by tradition and competition. His cognitive pattern was to refuse inherited solutions when his own perception contradicted them, even at the cost of lost work and lost momentum. The reversal reveals his hierarchy: observed physical truth outranks design commitment, patron expectation, and competitive precedent.
outcome: Leonardo proceeded with the pacing horse design, eventually building an enormous clay model. The bronze was never cast; the metal was diverted to cannons during the French invasion, and French soldiers later used the clay model for target practice.

decision: Leonardo dissected more than ten human bodies himself rather than trust existing anatomical authority or observed demonstrations.
context: Renaissance anatomical knowledge was primarily derived from Galen's ancient texts and occasional public dissections. A painter who wanted anatomical accuracy could attend a university dissection or study existing anatomical illustrations. Leonardo chose instead to personally dissect over ten corpses, destroying surrounding tissue layer by layer to isolate individual veins, and repeated the entire program twice to detect variation between bodies. This required access to cadavers (legally and socially fraught), tolerance for extreme working conditions, and hundreds of hours of labor.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Leonardo noticed that a single dissection, even if personally witnessed, could not reveal the systematic structure of the body because overlapping tissues obscured the deep architecture. He also noticed that bodies varied from one another, meaning a single specimen could not establish reliable knowledge. These are cues about sample size and observational interference that a conventional painter-anatomist would not have weighted, since their goal was surface accuracy rather than structural understanding.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Leonardo's surface goal was to paint more accurate nude figures. His deeper operative goal was to build a complete mechanical model of the human body that explained why muscles moved the way they did, not just what they looked like. He was pursuing explanatory anatomy, not descriptive anatomy, which required understanding load paths, lever systems, and fluid dynamics within the body.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: First, attending a professional dissection and sketching what he saw: rejected because a single public dissection could not isolate individual structures or reveal variation. The criterion was completeness of structural understanding. Second, relying on Galenic texts and existing anatomical illustrations: rejected because Leonardo had found them to be 'falsely understood,' and his epistemic standard required personal verification. Third, hiring a physician to dissect while Leonardo drew: rejected because the layer-by-layer destruction required real-time judgment about what to isolate next, which only Leonardo's own questions could guide.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: A conventional painter would have framed anatomy as a reference problem: learn enough to draw convincing bodies. Leonardo reframed it as a reverse-engineering problem: disassemble the machine until you understand its operating principles. His mental model was mechanical engineering applied to biology.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Leonardo expected to produce drawings that would make written anatomical authority obsolete, replacing text-based description with visual demonstration of three-dimensional structure. He accepted the enormous time cost and the grim working conditions as necessary inputs.

counterfactual: A competent Renaissance painter like Pollaiuolo or Signorelli, both known for anatomical interest, would have attended available dissections, studied surface musculature on live models, and supplemented with existing anatomical texts. The idea of personally dissecting ten bodies and repeating the process would have seemed obsessive and outside the painter's role.
divergence_explanation: Leonardo's decision diverged because his threshold for 'knowing' was set at mechanical understanding, not visual familiarity. Where a conventional painter needed to know what a muscle looked like, Leonardo needed to know why it was shaped that way, what forces acted on it, and how it varied across individuals. This reflects his foundational cognitive pattern: treating every domain as an engineering problem where surface description is insufficient and only structural comprehension counts as knowledge.
outcome: Leonardo produced anatomical drawings of unprecedented accuracy and explanatory power, anticipating findings that would not be formally established for centuries. The work was never published in his lifetime due to his mirror-script notation and the unfinished state of the manuscripts.

decision: Leonardo refused to hurry the Adoration of the Magi altarpiece despite contractual deadlines, did side work for the monks to buy time, and ultimately abandoned the painting unfinished.
context: The monks of San Donato a Scopeto commissioned Leonardo to paint an Adoration of the Magi with a strict thirty-month deadline. The contract was unusually punitive: Leonardo had to pay for his own colors and gold, and failure to deliver meant forfeiting all work done. He spent nearly a year on research and preparatory studies before beginning to paint, producing an extraordinarily complex composition with numerous elaborate figures. The monks grew impatient. Leonardo did odd jobs around the cloister (repainting their clock) to placate them, but never delivered the finished painting.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Leonardo noticed compositional and technical problems in the Adoration that his research kept revealing as deeper than initially apparent. Where a conventional painter would have noticed that the deadline was approaching and adjusted scope, Leonardo noticed that the painting's requirements kept expanding as his understanding deepened. He attended to the gap between his current conception and the ideal, treating that gap as the primary signal rather than the contractual timeline.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Leonardo's surface goal was to produce a great altarpiece. His operative goal was to solve the compositional and expressive problems the subject presented to his satisfaction, with delivery as a secondary consideration. His deepest structural objective was to maintain his standard of investigation: he would not ship a painting that embodied less understanding than he believed was achievable.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: First, simplifying the composition to meet the deadline: rejected because Leonardo had already conceived a complex multi-figure arrangement and refused to reduce it. The criterion was conceptual ambition over contractual compliance. Second, finishing the painting to a 'good enough' standard: rejected because Leonardo's quality threshold was set by his own evolving understanding, not by the patron's expectations. Third, renegotiating the contract for more time: Leonardo chose the indirect strategy of doing side work instead, suggesting he recognized the monks would not formally extend but might tolerate delay if placated.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: A conventional painter would have framed this as a delivery problem with a fixed deadline and escalating penalties. Leonardo reframed it as a research problem with an open-ended investigation timeline. His mental model treated the commission as an occasion for inquiry rather than a transaction to be completed.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Leonardo likely expected to eventually finish the painting but continually underestimated how long his investigations would take. He was calibrated to the possibility of abandonment, as his behavior shows he prioritized the investigation process over the delivery outcome.

counterfactual: Filippino Lippi, who eventually received the commission, would have planned a composition within his known capabilities, executed it within the contractual timeline, and delivered a competent altarpiece. He might have been less innovative but would have fulfilled the obligation and maintained his professional reputation.
divergence_explanation: Leonardo's decision diverged because he treated commissions as research programs rather than delivery contracts. His cognitive pattern was to let the scope of investigation expand to match the complexity he perceived in the subject, regardless of external time constraints. This reveals a fundamental priority inversion: where conventional painters optimized for completing the transaction, Leonardo optimized for exhausting the problem, accepting abandonment as a preferable outcome to premature closure.
outcome: The Adoration of the Magi was never finished. The incomplete panel, showing Leonardo's extraordinary compositional complexity, survived and is now in the Uffizi. It became one of the defining examples of Leonardo's pattern of non-completion.

decision: Leonardo answered humanist critics by adopting experience as his sole citation rather than competing on their terms with quoted authorities.
context: Renaissance intellectual culture was dominated by humanist scholars who established credibility through classical citations. Leonardo, largely self-taught and lacking formal Latin education, faced anticipated attacks from these lettered men who would dismiss his work as unscholarly. The conventional defensive strategy for an autodidact was to acquire classical references and pad one's work with quoted authorities to gain legitimacy. Leonardo had the option to do this but explicitly refused.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Leonardo noticed that his critics' authority derived from citation chains rather than from personal discovery. He perceived that the humanists were 'puffed up and pompous, dressed and decorated with the fruits not of their own labours but of those of others.' This is a structural observation about the difference between inherited knowledge and generated knowledge that a conventional respondent would not have articulated, since the citation system was the unquestioned infrastructure of scholarly legitimacy.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Leonardo's surface goal was to defend the legitimacy of his written work. His operative goal was to establish a new epistemic standard in which direct experience outranked textual authority. His deepest structural objective was to protect the integrity of his method: if he started citing authorities, he would be implicitly conceding that authority-citation was the correct standard of evidence.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: First, learning Latin and acquiring classical references to cite: rejected because this would have conceded the humanists' epistemic framework as legitimate, undermining his own experiential method. The criterion was methodological consistency over social acceptance. Second, ignoring the critics entirely: rejected because Leonardo felt the need to articulate why his approach was superior, not just different. Third, finding allies among other empirically-minded thinkers: Leonardo chose to stake his position alone, framing it as a personal epistemic commitment.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: A conventional autodidact would have framed this as a credibility problem to be solved by acquiring the missing credentials. Leonardo reframed it as a contest between two epistemologies: citation of masters versus direct consultation of nature. His mental model positioned the humanists not as gatekeepers to be appeased but as epistemically confused practitioners who mistook secondhand knowledge for understanding.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Leonardo expected continued social marginalization from the humanist establishment. He accepted this cost because he believed the experiential method would produce knowledge that citation-based scholarship could not, and that the work itself would eventually vindicate the approach.

counterfactual: A competent but convention-following Renaissance polymath like Alberti would have framed his empirical work within classical references, citing Aristotle on observation and Vitruvius on engineering to give his findings scholarly respectability. This was the standard strategy for bridging practical knowledge and academic legitimacy.
divergence_explanation: Leonardo's decision diverged because he perceived the citation system not as a neutral tool for establishing truth but as a self-reinforcing authority structure that actively obstructed discovery. His cognitive pattern was to trace credibility claims back to their epistemic foundations and reject any foundation that did not terminate in direct observation. This is the same pattern he applied to Galenic anatomy and Ptolemaic cosmology: inherited authority was treated as a hypothesis to be tested, not a baseline to be cited.
outcome: Leonardo's works circulated without classical scholarly apparatus and were largely ignored by the humanist establishment during his lifetime. His empirical method was vindicated centuries later when the notebooks were finally studied.

decision: Leonardo deliberately adopted the practice of staring at stained walls and random patterns to generate compositional ideas, despite acknowledging it looked ridiculous.
context: Renaissance composition was taught through the study of classical models, master drawings, and systematic perspective construction. Leonardo developed an alternative technique: studying dirty walls, veined marble, and random natural patterns to find landscapes, battles, and figures in the visual noise. He explicitly acknowledged this method appeared 'trifling, and almost laughable' to conventional practitioners. The method had no precedent in workshop pedagogy and invited mockery.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Leonardo noticed that random visual patterns contained compositional possibilities that deliberate construction could not reach. He attended to the generative potential of ambiguity itself, seeing that when the mind encounters unstructured visual information, it projects compositional solutions that bypass the constraints of deliberate planning. This is a perceptual cue about the relationship between noise and invention that no conventional painter would have weighted, since workshop training emphasized the elimination of randomness, not its exploitation.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Leonardo's surface goal was to find new compositional ideas. His operative goal was to escape the ceiling imposed by conventional compositional methods, which recycled known arrangements. His deeper structural objective was to exploit a specific cognitive mechanism: the brain's pattern-completion tendency, which generates novel forms when given ambiguous input.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: First, studying master compositions and recombining their elements: rejected because this recycled existing solutions and could not produce genuinely novel arrangements. The criterion was originality of output. Second, systematic geometric construction of compositions: rejected because this produced correct but predictable results. Third, dismissing the stained-wall technique as beneath serious practice: Leonardo explicitly refused this, defending the technique's utility despite its apparent absurdity.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: A conventional painter would have framed compositional invention as a craft problem solvable through study and practice within established methods. Leonardo reframed it as a perceptual-cognitive problem: how to generate forms the deliberate mind cannot reach. His mental model treated the brain as a pattern-completion engine that could be deliberately fed ambiguous input to produce novel output.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Leonardo expected the technique to produce compositional ideas unavailable through conventional methods. He was willing to be laughed at as the cost of accessing a richer space of possibilities.

counterfactual: A competent Renaissance painter like Raphael would have generated compositions through systematic study of classical arrangements, perspective grids, and the recombination of proven figurative elements. Using dirty walls as creative input would have seemed like an admission of creative bankruptcy.
divergence_explanation: Leonardo's decision diverged because he understood creativity as a computational process that could be hacked through input manipulation rather than through harder deliberate effort. His cognitive pattern was to treat apparent randomness as information-rich and to design procedures that exploited the brain's involuntary pattern-recognition. This is the same structural habit visible in his use of mirrors for self-critique: engineering external conditions to produce cognitive outputs that willpower alone cannot achieve.
outcome: The technique became one of Leonardo's documented creative methods and influenced later artistic and psychological thinking about the role of ambiguity in creative perception. It anticipated modern concepts of pareidolia and stochastic creative processes.

decision: Leonardo defended extended contemplation as legitimate work to the Duke of Milan when the Prior of Santa Maria delle Grazie complained about his apparent idleness on the Last Supper.
context: The Prior of the monastery hosting the Last Supper commission complained to the Duke that Leonardo would stand before the painting for half a day without touching a brush, or disappear for days to wander the streets. The Duke summoned Leonardo for an explanation. Leonardo was under real threat of losing the commission and the Duke's favor. The conventional response was to apologize and promise to work faster. Instead, Leonardo lectured the Duke on the nature of genius, arguing that the greatest work happens when the painter appears to work least.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Leonardo noticed that the Prior was measuring productivity by visible output (brush strokes per hour) rather than by the quality of the conception being developed. He also noticed an opportunity to reframe the conversation: rather than defending against the accusation of laziness, he could educate the Duke about the actual production process of great art. He further noticed that Judas's face remained unresolved and that the Prior's importunity provided a darkly comic solution.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Leonardo's surface goal was to retain the commission. His operative goal was to establish a precedent that his working method, including long periods of apparent inactivity, was legitimate and non-negotiable. His deepest structural objective was to protect the conception phase of creative work from being compressed by patron pressure, establishing that mental work counted as work.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: First, apologizing and increasing visible work hours: rejected because this would have subordinated his method to the Prior's productivity metric, setting a precedent for future interference. The criterion was methodological autonomy. Second, working faster on the painting by accepting lower-quality solutions for difficult faces: rejected because Leonardo's standard for the Judas and Christ figures required extended contemplation. Third, quietly ignoring the complaint and hoping it would pass: rejected because the Duke's involvement made the issue political and required a direct response.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: The Prior framed Leonardo's behavior as labor shirking. Leonardo reframed it as a misunderstanding of how intellectual production works: 'men of lofty genius accomplish the most when they work the least, seeking out inventions with the mind.' His mental model distinguished sharply between manual execution (which looks like work) and conceptual development (which looks like idleness), and he insisted the latter was the rate-limiting step.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Leonardo expected the Duke to understand and protect his working method, which the Duke did. He also expected the Prior to be chastened by the implied threat that his face might serve as the model for Judas.

counterfactual: A competent but conventional painter like Perugino would have maintained steady visible progress on the fresco, resolving difficult faces with proven compositional formulas rather than risking patron displeasure through prolonged contemplation. The idea of lecturing a Duke about creative process would have seemed dangerously presumptuous.
divergence_explanation: Leonardo's decision diverged because he refused to separate the defense of his working method from the work itself. Where a conventional painter would have compartmentalized the political problem (pacify the patron) from the creative problem (paint the fresco), Leonardo treated them as the same problem: if the patron did not understand conception time, the work would be compromised. His cognitive pattern was to identify the root cause of the threat (a false model of creative production) and correct it directly rather than manage symptoms.
outcome: The Duke sided with Leonardo and told the Prior to stop interfering. Leonardo completed most of the Last Supper but reportedly left the head of Christ unfinished, judging himself unable to conceive the required divine beauty.

decision: Leonardo left the head of Christ unfinished in the Last Supper, judging himself incapable of conceiving the divine beauty required.
context: The Last Supper was nearly complete. Every other figure had been resolved, including Judas. The head of Christ remained the final challenge. Leonardo needed to depict a face that conveyed divine nature while being painted by a human hand. The conventional solution was to use established iconographic formulas for Christ's face. The painting was a major commission for a powerful patron, and leaving a central element incomplete was professionally dangerous.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Leonardo noticed that his own capacity for facial invention, which had succeeded for every human character in the painting, met its limit at the conceptual category of the divine. He attended to the gap between what he could conceive and what the subject required, and recognized this gap as unclosable through further effort. This is a metacognitive cue about the boundary of one's own capability that most artists would suppress or deny rather than acknowledge.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Leonardo's surface goal was to complete the painting. His operative goal was to maintain the internal consistency of the work: every other face had been resolved to his satisfaction through deep character study, and inserting a conventionally rendered Christ would have broken the standard. His deepest structural objective was to preserve the painting's integrity by refusing to fake a solution he could not genuinely conceive.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: First, using an established iconographic formula for Christ's face: rejected because it would have introduced a conventionally derived element into a painting where every other face was conceived from direct observation and character study. The criterion was internal consistency of method. Second, using a living model as he had for other figures: rejected because no human face could embody the divine quality he believed the subject required. Third, continuing to work on the face indefinitely: rejected because Leonardo recognized the problem as conceptual, not technical, meaning more time would not close the gap.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: A conventional painter would have framed the unfinished Christ as a production problem to be solved before delivery. Leonardo framed it as an epistemological limit: there are things the human mind cannot conceive, and pretending otherwise would corrupt the work. His mental model treated artistic production as bounded by genuine understanding, not by technical skill.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Leonardo expected the incomplete Christ to be perceived as a deliberate statement about the limits of human art rather than as a failure of execution. He was calibrated to the reputational risk and chose principled incompletion over false resolution.

counterfactual: Any competent workshop painter would have completed Christ's face using the well-established Byzantine and Gothic iconographic traditions, producing a dignified if conventional divine countenance. The idea of leaving the central figure incomplete in a delivered commission would have been unthinkable.
divergence_explanation: Leonardo's decision diverged because he applied his own quality standard recursively: having raised the standard for every other figure through deep character study, he could not exempt the most important figure. His cognitive pattern was absolute consistency of method regardless of practical consequences. Where a conventional painter would have allowed the central figure to be handled differently (because it was sacred, not secular), Leonardo insisted that his empirical method applied universally, and when it reached its limit, he stopped rather than switch to a lesser method.
outcome: The Last Supper was delivered with Christ's head in an incomplete or minimally resolved state. The painting was nevertheless recognized immediately as a masterpiece and 'ever been held by the Milanese in the greatest veneration.'

decision: Leonardo accepted Cesare Borgia's offer to serve as architect and military engineer despite knowing Borgia's reputation for murder and deception.
context: After the fall of Ludovico Sforza, Leonardo was without a patron. His equestrian monument had been destroyed by French archers. He was over fifty with a long record of unfinished projects. Cesare Borgia, who had fought, murdered, and deceived his way to power across the Romagna, offered Leonardo a position as architect and general engineer. Leonardo had reflected that 'like the duke, nothing of his own had been completed either.' The conventional alternative was to seek a less morally compromised patron, such as the Florentine republic or the Venetian state.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Leonardo noticed a parallel between Ludovico's fate and his own: both had lost what they had built, both had left enterprises incomplete. He attended to the structural similarity between political and creative failure, using it as a prompt for action rather than reflection. He also noticed that Borgia's campaigns involved massive engineering challenges (fortifications, logistics, territorial administration) that offered the kind of large-scale applied problems Leonardo craved.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Leonardo's surface goal was to secure income and patronage. His operative goal was to finally complete something, to break the pattern of abandonment that had defined his career. His deepest structural objective was to gain access to real-world engineering problems at military scale, which offered a different completion logic than art commissions: a bridge either stands or it doesn't.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: First, returning to Florence to seek republican patronage: rejected because Florence offered painting commissions but not the engineering-scale problems Leonardo needed. The criterion was problem-scale, not moral safety. Second, approaching Venice or another Italian state: rejected because Borgia's campaigns were the most active theater of engineering operations in Italy. Third, refusing on moral grounds and continuing independent work: rejected because Leonardo's financial situation was desperate and his need for a patron with active projects overrode moral reservation.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: A conventional artist or engineer would have framed Borgia's offer as a moral decision: serve a tyrant or maintain integrity. Leonardo reframed it as a career-trajectory decision: the question was not whether Borgia was good but whether proximity to his campaigns would produce the conditions for completed work and real-world engineering application.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Leonardo expected to gain access to active military engineering projects and to finally see his designs built. He was calibrated to the moral cost and rationalized it through the parallel with his own and Ludovico's shared pattern of incompletion.

counterfactual: A competent engineer with conventional moral reasoning would have sought a less dangerous patron or negotiated terms that limited exposure to Borgia's worst activities. Serving a figure widely known for treachery and murder carried enormous reputational and physical risk.
divergence_explanation: Leonardo's decision diverged because he evaluated patronage opportunities primarily by the scale and nature of the problems they offered access to, not by the moral character of the patron. His cognitive pattern was instrumental: he treated patrons as enabling conditions for his work rather than as moral partners. The Borgia decision also reveals the pressure of accumulated non-completion, which shifted Leonardo's calculus toward any opportunity that might break the pattern, even at ethical cost.
outcome: Leonardo served Borgia for approximately a year, producing maps, fortification designs, and engineering surveys of the Romagna. He departed when Borgia's fortunes began to decline, having completed some practical engineering work but no lasting monuments.

decision: Leonardo proposed permanently diverting the Arno River to defeat Pisa rather than offering any conventional military plan.
context: Florence was at war with Pisa and unable to achieve a decisive victory through conventional military means. Machiavelli and the Signoria consulted Leonardo for a military plan. The conventional expectation was a tactical proposal: siege tactics, troop movements, or fortification designs. Instead, Leonardo proposed a monumental hydraulic engineering project: diverting the entire Arno River into two new canals that would empty into the sea south of Pisa, permanently cutting off the city's water supply and sea access.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Leonardo noticed that Pisa's power depended on its relationship with the Arno, specifically water supply and river-based sea access. Where military advisors attended to troop positions and fortifications, Leonardo attended to the infrastructural dependency that made Pisa viable as a city. He perceived the war not as a contest of armies but as a systems-dependency problem that could be resolved by modifying the underlying infrastructure.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Leonardo's surface goal was to help Florence defeat Pisa. His operative goal was to solve the problem at a level that made the military question irrelevant: if Pisa had no water and no sea access, military victory would be unnecessary. His deepest structural objective was to demonstrate that engineering could replace warfare, solving political problems through infrastructure modification rather than violence.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: First, a conventional siege plan with troop dispositions: rejected because Leonardo did not think in terms of temporary battles or tactical victories. The criterion was permanence of solution. Second, a more modest water-disruption plan (poisoning wells, temporary damming): rejected because Leonardo's engineering ambition demanded a complete, permanent restructuring. Third, advising that the war could not be won by engineering alone: rejected because Leonardo genuinely believed the river diversion was feasible.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Military advisors framed the Pisan problem as a war to be won through superior force or tactics. Leonardo framed it as an engineering problem: Pisa's viability depended on a specific geographic and hydrological configuration that could be permanently altered. His mental model treated geography as modifiable infrastructure rather than fixed terrain.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Leonardo expected the canal project to render Pisa defenseless without a single battle. He likely also envisioned the canals serving peacetime commercial and agricultural purposes, multiplying the return on the military investment.

counterfactual: A competent military engineer like the condottiero leaders Florence employed would have proposed siege works, artillery positions, or a blockade strategy. The idea of diverting an entire river as a military strategy would have seemed absurdly ambitious and impractical.
divergence_explanation: Leonardo's decision diverged because he habitually reframed tactical problems as infrastructure problems. His cognitive pattern was to identify the systemic dependency that sustained the adversary's position and modify it at the structural level rather than contest it at the tactical level. This is the same pattern visible in his urban design (restructure the city to eliminate plague) and his church design (restructure the building to optimize acoustics): find the physical substrate of the problem and redesign it.
outcome: The project was actually attempted but failed due to inadequate implementation: the canal digging was done by unskilled laborers who deviated from Leonardo's plans, and the channels were too shallow to divert the river. The failure was one of execution, not conception.

decision: Leonardo experimented with a novel oil-based wall binder on the Battle of Anghiari mural rather than using proven fresco technique, then abandoned the work when the experiment failed publicly.
context: Piero Soderini commissioned Leonardo to paint the Battle of Anghiari in the Great Council Chamber of Florence, directly opposite where Michelangelo would paint the Battle of Cascina. This was the most prestigious civic commission in Italy, with maximum public visibility. Established fresco technique (painting onto wet plaster) was well understood and reliable. Leonardo chose instead to experiment with a new oil-based composition for the wall binder. When the paint began to fail, he attempted to force-dry it with a specially built fire. The colors melted and ran down the wall in front of spectators.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Leonardo noticed that traditional fresco technique limited the painter's ability to achieve the tonal subtlety and rich coloring he wanted for a battle scene. He attended to the gap between what fresco could deliver and what his artistic vision required. A conventional fresco painter would have noticed the same limitation but accepted it as a constraint of the medium. Leonardo also noticed that oil painting on panels produced superior color effects but had not been adapted for walls.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Leonardo's surface goal was to paint a masterpiece that would outshine Michelangelo's adjacent work. His operative goal was to solve the technical problem of achieving oil-painting effects on a wall surface, which required inventing a new binding medium. His deepest structural objective was to refuse the constraints of existing technique when those constraints conflicted with his artistic vision.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: First, using proven buon fresco technique: rejected because it could not achieve the tonal range Leonardo wanted. The criterion was artistic capability over technical safety. Second, adapting his approach to work within fresco's limitations: rejected because Leonardo viewed the medium as a means to an end, not a constraint to be respected. Third, testing the new binder on a small section first before committing the entire wall: possibly attempted but inadequately, revealing overconfidence in the experimental method.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: A conventional fresco painter would have framed this as an execution problem: how to paint a great battle scene using proven technique. Leonardo framed it as a materials-science problem: how to get oil-painting quality on a wall surface. His mental model treated the commission as a dual problem, artistic and technical, and he was unwilling to compromise on either dimension.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Leonardo expected the oil-binder experiment to produce a mural with unprecedented color richness and tonal subtlety. When the paint failed to dry, he gambled on the fire-drying intervention, expecting heat to accelerate the curing process. He was not calibrated to the catastrophic failure mode.

counterfactual: Michelangelo, working on the adjacent wall, used established fresco technique. Any competent fresco painter would have done the same on such a high-stakes commission, reserving technical experiments for lower-stakes projects. The risk-reward calculus of experimenting on the most visible wall in Florence would have been obviously unfavorable.
divergence_explanation: Leonardo's decision diverged because he was constitutionally unable to accept a known technical limitation when he believed a better solution was theoretically possible. His cognitive pattern was to treat every commission as simultaneously an artistic and a technical-research opportunity. The failure reveals the framework's limit: when the research component of a commission fails, the artistic component fails with it, because Leonardo refused to decouple the two.
outcome: The paint melted and ran down the wall in a public disaster. Leonardo abandoned the commission. The surviving portion of the painting was eventually covered over by Vasari's later frescoes. It became the most famous example of Leonardo's experimental risk-taking destroying a major work.

decision: Leonardo spent four years refining the Mona Lisa, employed musicians to maintain the sitter's expression, and ultimately kept the painting rather than delivering it to the patron.
context: Francesco del Giocondo commissioned a portrait of his wife, Monna Lisa. Portraits were typically completed in weeks or months and delivered as commercial transactions. Leonardo worked on this portrait for four years, hiring musicians and jesters to keep the sitter's expression alive during sessions. He employed unprecedented techniques of sfumato to achieve atmospheric depth. When he left Florence, he took the painting with him rather than delivering it, eventually carrying it to France where it entered the royal collection.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Leonardo noticed that conventional portraiture captured a static mask rather than a living expression. He attended to the problem of how a face changes when the subject becomes bored or melancholy during long sitting sessions, and devised the environmental solution of musicians and entertainers. He also noticed that the techniques he was developing (sfumato, atmospheric perspective applied to faces) kept revealing new possibilities that justified continued work.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Leonardo's surface goal was to paint a portrait. His operative goal was to solve the problem of depicting a living human expression rather than a frozen pose, which required both environmental staging (the musicians) and technical innovation (sfumato). His deepest structural objective was to use this commission as a vehicle for developing painting techniques that transcended the portrait genre.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: First, completing the portrait in a standard timeframe and delivering it: rejected because Leonardo's evolving technique kept revealing new possibilities. The criterion was artistic discovery over commercial obligation. Second, working faster by accepting a less technically ambitious approach: rejected because the sfumato technique was the point of the exercise. Third, declaring the painting finished at any of several possible stopping points: rejected because Leonardo did not recognize a natural completion point when the technique itself was still developing.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: A conventional portraitist would have framed this as a commercial transaction: paint the wife, deliver the picture, collect payment. Leonardo framed it as a research program in the depiction of living expression, for which the portrait was merely the experimental substrate. His mental model treated the commission as an open-ended investigation rather than a bounded deliverable.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Leonardo expected to develop techniques that would permanently advance the art of portraiture. He was calibrated to the cost of never delivering the painting, ultimately choosing to keep it as a continuing work rather than release it in an incomplete state.

counterfactual: A competent Florentine portraitist like Ghirlandaio would have completed the portrait within a few months, producing an excellent likeness using established techniques, and delivered it to the commissioning husband. The idea of keeping a client's portrait for years and then absconding with it would have been professionally suicidal.
divergence_explanation: Leonardo's decision diverged because he could not distinguish between 'completing a painting' and 'exhausting the technical possibilities the painting revealed.' His cognitive pattern was to treat each painting as a window into a set of unsolved technical problems, and to continue working as long as new problems kept appearing. The Mona Lisa became a lifelong companion rather than a delivered product because the research it enabled never reached a natural stopping point.
outcome: The Mona Lisa was never delivered to Francesco del Giocondo. Leonardo carried it to France where it entered the collection of King Francis I. It became the most famous painting in the world, and the sfumato technique Leonardo developed through it became a defining achievement of Western art.

decision: Leonardo demanded that painters learn anatomy through personal dissection as a precondition for painting the nude, rejecting surface observation as insufficient.
context: Renaissance painter training focused on drawing from live models and antique sculptures to learn the human form. Anatomical knowledge was considered the province of physicians, not painters. Leonardo insisted that painters must personally learn 'the sinews, bones, muscles and tendons' through dissection, ridiculing painters who skipped this step as producing figures that looked like 'a sack of walnuts' or 'a bundle of radishes.' He was proposing a fundamental restructuring of the painter's education to include a discipline from an entirely different profession.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Leonardo noticed a specific visual failure in contemporary painting: figures that showed surface musculature without understanding the underlying mechanical structure. He attended to the difference between surface pattern (what a muscle looks like from outside) and structural mechanism (why the muscle has that shape, what forces act on it). This is a cross-domain cue that requires understanding both painting and mechanics to detect.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Leonardo's surface goal was to improve the quality of figure painting. His operative goal was to establish that painting required engineering-level understanding of the human body, not just visual familiarity. His deepest structural objective was to eliminate the boundary between painting and anatomy, making cross-domain knowledge the standard rather than the exception.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: First, improving figure drawing through better observation of surface anatomy on live models: rejected because surface observation could not reveal the mechanical reasons for bodily form. The criterion was causal understanding, not visual accuracy. Second, studying existing anatomical texts and illustrations: rejected because these were verbal descriptions that did not translate directly into painterly knowledge. Third, accepting that some stylization of the figure was artistically valid: rejected because Leonardo's standard for figure painting was mechanical truth, not aesthetic convention.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: A conventional painter would have framed nude painting as a draftsmanship problem: how to accurately render the external form. Leonardo reframed it as an engineering problem: how to depict a mechanical system (the body) so that every visible surface reflects the underlying causal structure. His mental model treated the human body as a machine that could only be drawn correctly if its operating principles were understood.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Leonardo expected this approach to produce figures of unprecedented naturalism and anatomical truth. He accepted that few painters would undertake the labor of dissection, effectively proposing an elite standard that most would fail to meet.

counterfactual: A competent Renaissance painter like Andrea del Sarto would have studied anatomy through life drawing and perhaps a few observed dissections, producing excellent figures based on external observation. The idea that a painter should personally dissect corpses would have seemed an unnecessary and gruesome detour from the actual work of painting.
divergence_explanation: Leonardo's decision diverged because he applied his universal principle, that understanding requires knowing causes rather than surfaces, to the specific domain of figure painting. His cognitive pattern was to treat any visual phenomenon as the output of a mechanical system that must be understood before it can be faithfully depicted. This cross-domain transfer (engineering principles applied to artistic training) is the signature move that distinguishes his pedagogy from all contemporary alternatives.
outcome: Leonardo's anatomical drawings became the most detailed and accurate of his era, and his figure painting achieved unprecedented naturalism. However, his pedagogical demand was too extreme for most painters, and the integration of anatomy into standard painter training happened only gradually over the following centuries.

decision: Leonardo chose to use the mirror as a systematic de-biasing tool for his own painting, treating it as a 'silent external critic' rather than a mere checking device.
context: Painters routinely checked their work by stepping back or comparing it to the model. Leonardo elevated the flat mirror to the status of 'true master of painters,' using it both to benchmark his work against reflected reality and to view his own work reversed, 'as the work of another hand.' The technique exploited the psychological effect of unfamiliarity to break through the painter's habitual blindness to his own errors. He also prescribed taking breaks for the same reason.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Leonardo noticed a specific cognitive failure: the painter's judgment degrades through prolonged exposure to his own work, causing errors to become invisible through familiarity. This is a metacognitive observation about adaptation and perceptual blindness. He noticed that reversing the image mechanically restored the ability to see errors because the reversed version was processed as if it were someone else's work. A conventional painter might have noticed that stepping back helped but would not have theorized the underlying mechanism.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Leonardo's surface goal was to improve the accuracy of his painting. His operative goal was to engineer a reliable external check on his own perceptual biases, one that did not depend on willpower or self-discipline. His deepest structural objective was to design cognitive tools that mechanically corrected for known limitations of the human perceptual system.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: First, relying on self-discipline and careful looking to catch errors: rejected because Leonardo recognized that willpower could not overcome perceptual adaptation. The criterion was reliability of the error-detection mechanism. Second, seeking feedback from other painters: useful but not always available and subject to the other painter's own biases. Third, accepting that some errors were inevitable: rejected because Leonardo believed a mechanical technique could systematically reduce them.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: A conventional painter would have framed error detection as a skill problem: better painters catch more errors. Leonardo framed it as a systems-design problem: the human visual system has a known failure mode (familiarity-induced blindness) and the solution is to engineer around it with a tool (the mirror) that mechanically disrupts the failure mode.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Leonardo expected the mirror technique to reliably reveal errors that unaided perception could not detect. He treated it as a repeatable procedure, not a trick, prescribing it alongside regular breaks as part of an integrated error-reduction system.

counterfactual: A competent painter might occasionally glance at a mirror or ask a colleague for feedback, but would not have systematized the technique or theorized why it worked. The idea that your own judgment was systematically unreliable and needed mechanical correction would have seemed defeatist.
divergence_explanation: Leonardo's decision diverged because he treated his own cognitive limitations not as personal failings to be overcome through effort but as design flaws in the human perceptual system that required engineering solutions. His cognitive pattern was to externalize self-correction: rather than trying harder to see his own errors, he designed a tool that made errors mechanically visible. This is the same pattern as staring at stained walls, using external conditions to produce cognitive outputs that internal effort cannot reliably generate.
outcome: The mirror technique became one of Leonardo's standard prescribed practices for painters and was widely adopted in subsequent art pedagogy. It remains a common technique in art studios today.

decision: Leonardo wrote a covert letter to the Piacenza cathedral commission (under another patron's name) to sabotage rival bronze sculptors and nominate himself.
context: The cathedral of Piacenza was selecting a sculptor for bronze doors, a prestigious commission. Leonardo was already committed to the Sforza horse in Milan. Learning of the Piacenza opportunity, he drafted a letter designed to be sent under the name of a Milanese patron, warning the commissioners to slow their selection and mocking rival candidates as 'a potter, a maker of cuirasses, a bell-founder, a bell ringer, and a bombardier.' He then named himself as the only capable master, while simultaneously claiming (in third person) that he was too busy to care about the commission.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Leonardo noticed that the Piacenza commission was about to go to an inferior competitor and that direct self-nomination would look desperate or presumptuous. He attended to the political dynamics of the selection process: commissioners could be influenced by a credible third-party opinion more effectively than by a candidate's self-promotion. He also noticed the power of timing, urging the committee to slow down before committing.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Leonardo's surface goal was to win the commission. His operative goal was to undermine competitors while maintaining an appearance of disinterested superiority. His deepest structural objective was to establish his brand as the only legitimate master, using scarcity signaling ('he has work for all his life time') to increase perceived value.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: First, direct self-nomination with a portfolio: rejected because it would have appeared desperate and reduced his negotiating leverage. The criterion was perceived status. Second, ignoring the commission and focusing on the Sforza horse: rejected because Leonardo could not resist the opportunity even while overcommitted. Third, sending the letter under his own name: rejected because the ghost-writing strategy created plausible deniability and made the endorsement appear to come from an independent authority.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: A conventional sculptor would have framed this as a competition to be entered directly or ignored. Leonardo framed it as a reputation-management operation requiring covert influence, timing manipulation, and competitive intelligence. His mental model was political rather than meritocratic: the best sculptor does not automatically win; the best-positioned one does.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Leonardo expected the letter to delay the selection and create doubt about rival candidates, opening space for his eventual nomination. He was willing to accept the reputational risk of the ghostwriting being discovered.

counterfactual: A competent sculptor like Amadeo or Solari would have submitted a formal proposal with drawings and references, competing on the merits of their design. The idea of ghost-writing an attack letter under a patron's name would have seemed dishonorable and unnecessarily complex.
divergence_explanation: Leonardo's decision diverged because he treated the commission market as a strategic game rather than a meritocratic competition. His cognitive pattern was to model the decision-makers' psychology and design interventions that shaped their perception before they evaluated any artwork. This reveals a layer of Leonardo's intelligence that is rarely discussed: his sophisticated understanding of social influence and his willingness to deploy covert tactics when direct competition seemed insufficient.
outcome: The historical record does not show Leonardo winning the Piacenza commission. The letter survives as evidence of his strategic maneuvering and his assessment of his own competitors.

decision: Leonardo designed a two-tiered city with separated traffic levels explicitly as an epidemiological intervention after witnessing plague conditions.
context: Milan was suffering from recurrent plague, which Leonardo attributed to overcrowding: people 'stand like goats one behind the other, filling every place with fetid smells and sowing seeds of pestilence and death.' The conventional responses to plague were quarantine, prayer, and flight. Leonardo instead proposed a fundamental urban redesign: dispersing the population into ten satellite towns of five thousand houses each, with high streets for pedestrians and low streets for carts, waste, and animals, connected by staircases at every arch.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Leonardo noticed that plague was not a spiritual or random affliction but a consequence of physical density and contamination. He attended to the causal chain from population density through waste accumulation to disease transmission, a connection that would not be formally established for centuries. He also noticed that the political benefits of urban redesign (beauty, revenue, fame for the prince) could be bundled with the public-health argument to make the proposal politically viable.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Leonardo's surface goal was to reduce plague mortality. His operative goal was to redesign the physical infrastructure of urban life so that the conditions producing plague could not recur. His deepest structural objective was to demonstrate that environmental design could solve problems conventionally attributed to divine punishment or personal sin.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: First, improving quarantine protocols within the existing urban layout: rejected because quarantine treated symptoms rather than causes. The criterion was root-cause elimination. Second, building a single new city: rejected because Leonardo proposed distributing the population across ten towns, revealing his understanding that the key variable was density, not location. Third, proposing sanitation improvements without restructuring: rejected because Leonardo's analysis showed the street layout itself was the disease vector.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: A conventional urban advisor would have framed plague as a medical or spiritual problem requiring doctors, priests, and quarantine. Leonardo framed it as an architectural problem: the city's physical design created the conditions for plague, so only a physical redesign could prevent it. His mental model treated disease as an engineering failure of the built environment.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Leonardo expected the multi-town, two-tier design to eliminate the overcrowding that caused plague while also producing aesthetic, economic, and political benefits for the prince. He packaged the proposal as a multi-benefit investment rather than a pure public-health measure.

counterfactual: A competent Renaissance architect like Filarete would have proposed improvements within existing urban forms: wider streets, better drainage, more open spaces. The idea of dispersing an entire urban population into ten new towns with two-level street systems would have seemed utopian and impractical.
divergence_explanation: Leonardo's decision diverged because he traced the plague problem back to its physical root cause (density and waste concentration) rather than accepting the conventional framing as a medical or divine problem. His cognitive pattern was to find the infrastructural substrate of any problem and redesign it, treating symptoms as evidence of design failure. The multi-benefit packaging (health, beauty, revenue, fame) reveals his habit of making radical proposals politically viable by attaching them to the patron's existing incentive structure.
outcome: The two-tier city was never built. Leonardo's urban designs survived only in his notebooks and were recognized centuries later as anticipating modern principles of urban planning, traffic separation, and public health engineering.

decision: Leonardo transformed a peasant's crude wooden buckler into a terrifying Medusa composition by filling a private room with decomposing animals and composing from them.
context: Leonardo's father brought him a rough wooden shield that a peasant had asked to have painted. The conventional approach was to paint a standard heraldic device or decorative motif appropriate to a rustic object. Leonardo first reshaped the crude buckler on a lathe, then locked himself in a private room with lizards, bats, crickets, serpents, butterflies, and grasshoppers, composing from their parts an imaginary monster of terrifying realism. He worked so long that the dead animals stank unbearably, but he 'did not notice it, so great was the love that he bore towards art.'

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Leonardo noticed that the peasant's buckler was an opportunity to solve a compositional problem: how to create a convincing monster from real anatomical parts. He attended to the functional requirement of a shield (to terrify the enemy) and treated it as a design brief for maximum psychological impact rather than as a minor decorative commission. He also noticed that real animal anatomy, recombined, would produce a more convincing monster than pure imagination.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Leonardo's surface goal was to paint a shield. His operative goal was to solve the problem of creating a believable impossible creature through empirical study of real anatomy. His deepest structural objective was to test whether his cross-domain method (studying real organisms to compose imaginary ones) could produce results that surpassed purely imaginative invention.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: First, painting a conventional heraldic device: rejected because the commission, however humble, presented an artistic problem Leonardo found interesting. The criterion was intellectual engagement, not proportionality of effort to payment. Second, inventing a monster from imagination alone: rejected because Leonardo's method required empirical reference even for imaginary subjects. Third, declining the minor commission: rejected because Leonardo saw in it a research opportunity that a prestigious commission might not have provided.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: A conventional painter would have framed this as a trivial job to be completed quickly and cheaply. Leonardo reframed it as an experiment in composite anatomy and psychological impact. His mental model treated every commission, regardless of scale, as a potential research vehicle.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Leonardo expected to produce a buckler painting so terrifyingly realistic that it would shock viewers. His father's reaction confirmed the success: he was startled by the image and Leonardo declared the shield had 'served the purpose for which it was made.'

counterfactual: A competent workshop painter would have spent an afternoon painting a standard device on the buckler and returned it. The idea of spending weeks in a room with decomposing animals to paint a peasant's shield would have seemed like insanity.
divergence_explanation: Leonardo's decision diverged because he was unable to calibrate effort to the social importance of the commission. His cognitive pattern was to respond to the intellectual interest of the problem rather than to its commercial or social significance. This reveals both the strength of his framework (every problem receives full analytical attention) and its limitation (no ability to triage effort by external importance, leading to massive overinvestment in trivial commissions and equal underdelivery on important ones).
outcome: The buckler was so convincing that Leonardo's father was frightened by it. Ser Piero sold it to a merchant for a hundred ducats, and it eventually reached the Duke of Milan for three hundred. The peasant received a replacement shield with a standard device.

decision: Leonardo arrived at the Sforza court bearing a silver lyre he had built in the shape of a horse's skull, winning favor through musical-engineering spectacle rather than artistic credentials.
context: Summoned to Milan to present himself to Ludovico Sforza, Leonardo could have arrived with paintings, engineering drawings, or recommendations from Florentine patrons. Instead, he chose to bring a bizarre silver lyre he had personally constructed in the form of a horse's skull, designed so that 'the harmony might be of greater volume and more sonorous in tone.' He used it to outperform all the court musicians. The decision to lead with an invented musical instrument rather than with his painting portfolio was a deliberate staging choice.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Leonardo noticed that a court introduction was a performance opportunity, not a portfolio review. He attended to the social dynamics of courtly favor: Ludovico would be more impressed by a novel experience than by a conventional presentation of credentials. He also noticed that the horse-skull lyre combined multiple attention-grabbing elements: visual novelty (bizarre form), acoustic superiority (better resonance), and competitive proof (outperforming court musicians in real time).
cdm_probes.active_goals: Leonardo's surface goal was to make a strong first impression at court. His operative goal was to demonstrate his unique capability, the integration of engineering, aesthetics, and performance, in a single object and event. His deepest structural objective was to establish himself as a category of one rather than as a competitor within an existing category.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: First, presenting a portfolio of paintings: rejected because this would have placed him in a crowded category with established competitors. The criterion was differentiation from all possible rivals. Second, presenting engineering drawings: rejected because drawings were passive and did not create the experiential impact of a live performance. Third, arriving conventionally and letting his reputation precede him: rejected because Leonardo's Florentine reputation was primarily as a painter, which was not the identity he wanted to establish in Milan.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: A conventional artist would have framed the court introduction as a credentialing event: demonstrate competence within an established category. Leonardo framed it as a theatrical experience: create a moment that no one else could replicate, establishing himself as uncategorizable.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Leonardo expected to win the Duke's curiosity and favor through the combined novelty and quality of the performance. He was calibrated to the risk that the bizarre instrument might seem ridiculous, but bet on the sonic quality vindicating the visual novelty.

counterfactual: A competent Florentine painter seeking Milanese patronage would have brought finished paintings or cartoons to demonstrate skill, perhaps with letters of recommendation from Medici associates.
